metadata.title: Why More Data Makes the Wrong Answer More Confident
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Computational Skepticism candidate 02. Source chapter: books/computational-skepticism-for-ai/chapters/03-bias-where-it-enters-and-who-is-responsible.md
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on real newsprint scan; desaturated archival plates; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines; hand-drawn annotation ring used once per graphic at most
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, dusty blue = the truth, terracotta = the biased estimate, ink = neutral/unbiased darts, gold highlighter as the single editor's-pen voice

metadata.color_semantics: dusty blue #5B7B9C = the truth (the real value); terracotta #D35F43 = the biased estimator's darts/cluster; ink = unbiased darts; never swap mid-film
metadata.note: Facts verified 2026-07-06 (FACTCHECK.md): Literary Digest 1936 — 10M ballots mailed to auto/telephone owners, ~2.4M returned, predicted Landon 57%/370 EV; FDR won ~61% and 46 of 48 states; Digest folded within 18 months (ceased 1938). Card exclusions: no bias-variance decomposition, at most ONE 'bias' label (no E[theta-hat] notation), no ten-mechanism taxonomy, Literary Digest history capped at the hook + one causal line.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B01: palette=claude but the cold open is 'un-annotated' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B11: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. The biggest poll in American history — two point four million responses — confidently predicted the loser. And more responses would have made it worse.

card.copy: Why more data makes the wrong answer more confident

beats.new_visual_element: [TITLE] more data, more confident, more wrong

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [TITLE] more data, more confident, more wrong

beats.narration_text: Nineteen thirty-six. The Literary Digest mails out ten million ballots, gets two point four million back, and calls the election for Alf Landon.
beats.scene_description: a 1936 newspaper front page or Literary Digest poll announcement, desaturated on the newsprint ground, slow push-in on the prediction headline
beats.image_prompt: 1936 American newspaper front page announcing a presidential straw poll prediction, aged newsprint reproduction, desaturated flat print, editorial collage

build.needs: YOU → drop a historical image at media/B02.png (or .mp4)

beats.narration_text: Franklin Roosevelt then won forty-six of the forty-eight states. The poll wasn't just wrong. It was enormously, confidently wrong.
beats.scene_description: the same prediction plate, smaller; the editor's pen strikes a terracotta X through the prediction as a charcoal serif label lands: 'Roosevelt: 46 of 48 states'

build.needs: YOU → drop a historical image at media/B03.png (or .mp4)
build.suggested: find: [ANNOTATE] the X through the prediction

beats.narration_text: Two point four million people. Surely a sample that big can't lie? That instinct — more data means closer to the truth — is the one this story breaks.

document.quote: A sample that big can't lie.

beats.scene_description: the instinct set large in charcoal serif; gold highlighter sweeps 'can't' as it is spoken

beats.narration_text: Here's the machine underneath. Say the truth is a single point — the electorate's real support. Every poll you run is one dart thrown at that point.

production_viz.label: the truth is a point; a poll is a dart
production_viz.mechanic: a target draws itself — two hairline concentric rings around a solid blue center dot labeled 'the truth'; three ink darts land near it
production_viz.note: abstract target — no candidate names, no percentages

beats.new_visual_element: [DIAGRAM] the target and the first darts

beats.narration_text: An unbiased poll scatters its darts around the truth. Small samples scatter wide. Big samples tighten. For an honest poll, more data really does mean closer.

production_viz.label: unbiased: the scatter shrinks around the truth
production_viz.mechanic: ink darts accumulate in waves of decreasing spread, all centered on the blue truth dot — the cloud visibly tightens onto the right place
production_viz.note: seeded scatter; spread shrinks with n — honest convergence

beats.new_visual_element: [ISOTYPE] the honest cloud tightens on the truth

viz.label: unbiased convergence
viz.note: waves of shrinking spread, centered on truth

beats.narration_text: But the Digest's darts weren't aimed at the truth. Its ballots went to people with cars and telephones — in nineteen thirty-six, the comfortable.
beats.scene_description: the target persists; a terracotta crosshair drifts from the blue truth dot to an offset aim-point; a small chip lands: 'who got ballots: car & telephone owners'

production_viz.mechanic: terracotta crosshair Transforms from the truth dot to the offset point; LabelChip with the sampling-frame line
production_viz.note: the one causal history line (card exclusion: Literary Digest capped at the hook + one line)

beats.new_visual_element: [ANNOTATE] the aim-point drifts off the truth

beats.narration_text: Now add data. The scatter tightens beautifully — around the wrong point. Two point four million darts make a tiny, precise, supremely confident cluster. Off-center.

production_viz.label: biased: the scatter shrinks around the wrong point
production_viz.mechanic: terracotta darts accumulate in waves of decreasing spread around the OFFSET aim-point while a mono counter ticks n = 100 → 10,000 → 2,400,000; the blue truth dot sits alone outside the tight cluster
production_viz.note: the film's core image — precision without correction

beats.narration_text: Scale bought precision. It never bought correction. That stubborn distance between the cluster and the truth has a name — and no amount of more-of-the-same touches it.
beats.scene_description: the end-state persists; a dumbbell line is drawn from the cluster's center to the blue truth dot, with the film's single label above it: 'the bias'; the editor's pen rings the gap

production_viz.mechanic: measured line between cluster center and truth dot; SerifLabel 'the bias' above (the ONE label the card allows); HandRing around the gap on 'name'
production_viz.note: no E[theta-hat] notation — one plain-language label only; one ring, the film's single ring

annotation.target: the gap between cluster and truth

beats.new_visual_element: [ANNOTATE] the gap gets its name — the bias

beats.narration_text: So when a huge sample agrees with itself, that is all you have learned: it agrees with itself. Precision is how tightly the darts cluster. Truth is where.

production_viz.label: precisely wrong vs roughly right
production_viz.mechanic: two small targets side by side: left, a tight terracotta cluster far from its blue center, labeled 'precisely wrong'; right, a loose ink cloud centered on its blue dot, labeled 'roughly right'
production_viz.note: the two-axis lesson: tightness vs location

beats.narration_text: The Literary Digest folded within two years. The lesson outlived it: more data shrinks your error bars. It does not shrink your blind spot.

card.sub: from Computational Skepticism for AI — chapter 3

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] endcard — scale buys precision, never correction

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] endcard — scale buys precision, never correction
